content.problemStatement:
When we use hashing, we are trading time for memory. This means we can make our code faster by using more memory.

In the first example:
The input values range from $1$ to $10^4$.
Since this range is small, we can easily create a hash array and solve the problem.
Now, imagine if the input values were as large as $1$ to $10^9$. This approach would fail because creating an array large enough to store all possible values (from $1$ to $10^9$) would use too much memory.

However, there’s something important to notice:

The number of distinct values in the array is always smaller than or equal to the size of the array.
If the array has **N** elements, there will be at most N distinct values.
#### Thought-Provoking Question:
How can we handle values as large as 10^9 with just an array of size **N** (around $10^5$)?
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 1000$
- $2 \leq N \leq 10^5$
- $1 \leq A_i \leq 10^9$
- The sum of $N$ across all test cases does not exceed $10^5$
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
- The first line of each test case contains a single integer $N$, denoting the length of array $A$.
- The second line of each test case contains $N$ space-separated integers $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$ — denoting the array $A$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the maximum value of $A_1+A_N$ you can get after several right rotations.
